text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY CONSULT NOTE
Metastatic Melanoma — Initial Staging and Treatment Planning

---

ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
DEPARTMENT: Medical Oncology
Reason for Consultation: New diagnosis of Stage IV metastatic melanoma, BRAF V600E-mutant; treatment planning

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Ms. Renata Okonkwo is a 55-year-old woman of West African and Irish descent who presents for initial oncology consultation following biopsy-confirmed Stage IV (M1c) metastatic melanoma arising from the mid-back. She reports a tender right axillary mass, pruritic cutaneous nodules on the trunk and arms, and progressive fatigue over the past several weeks.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okonkwo first noticed a slowly changing pigmented lesion on her mid-back approximately 8–10 months ago. She has a longstanding history of atypical/dysplastic nevi since her 30s and was followed by dermatology intermittently, though she had not had a full skin check in approximately 2 years. Over the past 3–4 months, she noted that the mid-back lesion had enlarged and developed irregular borders with a darker, more heterogeneous appearance. Approximately 4–6 weeks ago, she noticed a growing, tender right axillary mass, which prompted urgent evaluation. She also reports developing multiple new subcutaneous nodules on her trunk and bilateral upper extremities over a similar timeframe, several of which have become pruritic. She endorses significant fatigue and a mild decrease in energy, though she has maintained most of her daily activities. She denies fevers, night sweats, significant weight loss, chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, or any neurological symptoms.

She was referred for excisional biopsy of the primary mid-back lesion and concurrent core needle biopsy of the right axillary lymph node, both of which confirmed malignant melanoma, nodular type (see pathology report). Sentinel lymph node biopsy identified 3 of 4 right axillary nodes positive for metastatic melanoma with extranodal extension. A staging PET-CT and MRI brain were subsequently performed, confirming widespread metastatic disease (see below). Comprehensive genomic profiling via FoundationOne CDx was sent from the right axillary node biopsy specimen.

Current symptoms at time of this visit: tender right axillary fullness, multiple pruritic nodules on trunk and upper arms, fatigue (rates 4/10). Denies focal neurological deficits, visual changes, dyspnea, or jaundice. Bowel and bladder function normal; baseline stool frequency 1–2x/day.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Fatigue present, mild-moderate (4/10). Denies fever, chills, night sweats, unintentional weight loss.
HEENT: Denies headache, visual changes, diplopia. History of anterior uveitis (remote, resolved).
Respiratory: Denies dyspnea, hemoptysis, cough.
Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain, palpitations, lower extremity edema.
Gastrointestinal: Denies nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, constipation, blood per rectum. Baseline 1–2 BMs/day.
Musculoskeletal: Myalgias intermittent, mild.
Skin/Integumentary: Pruritic nodules on trunk and upper extremities, confirmed melanoma metastases. No new ulcerations or bleeding from lesions.
Neurological: Denies focal weakness, paresthesias, gait instability, seizures.
Psychiatric: Anxious about diagnosis, appropriate affect. Engaged and asking thoughtful questions.
Endocrine: Pre-existing hypothyroidism, well-controlled on levothyroxine per patient report.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Hypothyroidism — on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, diagnosed approximately 8 years ago, well-controlled
2. Multiple atypical/dysplastic nevi — followed by dermatology since her 30s, multiple prior shave biopsies with mildly atypical findings
3. Anterior uveitis — single episode approximately 12 years ago, resolved with topical steroids; no recurrence; followed by ophthalmology at the time
4. No prior malignancies
5. No prior surgeries beyond skin biopsies
6. No diabetes, hypertension, coronary artery disease, or pulmonary disease

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY
- Excisional biopsy of primary melanoma lesion (mid-back) — recent
- Core needle biopsy, right axillary lymph node — recent
- Sentinel lymph node biopsy, right axilla (3/4 nodes positive) — recent
- Core needle biopsy, subcutaneous abdominal wall nodule — recent
- Multiple prior punch/shave biopsies of atypical nevi (remote, no malignancy)

SOCIAL HISTORY
Ms. Okonkwo works full-time as a landscape architect, a physically active profession that involves outdoor exposure. She is a lifelong non-smoker. She reports occasional social alcohol use (1–2 drinks per week) and denies recreational drug use. She lives with her partner and has two adult children. She maintains an active lifestyle with regular walking and cycling. She has no significant sun-seeking behavior per se, though her profession involves outdoor UV exposure. She does not routinely use sunscreen. She is college-educated and engaged with her medical care.

FAMILY HISTORY
- No family history of melanoma or skin cancer
- Mother: breast cancer diagnosed in her 60s, survived
- Father: type 2 diabetes, hypertension
- One sibling: no known malignancies
- No family history of other cancers relevant to hereditary melanoma syndromes (pancreatic, ocular, renal)
- No known CDKN2A germline mutation in family (patient's germline testing negative for pathogenic variants per concurrent testing)

ALLERGIES
- Penicillin: rash (hives) — documented allergy
- No known allergies to sulfonamides, NSAIDs, or contrast media

MEDICATIONS
1. Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily (hypothyroidism)
2. Multivitamin PO daily
3. No anticoagulants, antiplatelet agents, or immunosuppressants
4. No herbal supplements or OTC medications beyond the above

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vitals: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, RR 16/min, Temperature 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 67 kg, Height 165 cm, BMI 24.6 kg/m²
Performance Status: ECOG 1 (restricted in physically strenuous activity but ambulatory and able to carry out work of a light or sedentary nature)

General: Alert, oriented, well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Anxious but engaged.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal and reactive bilaterally. No scleral icterus. No cervical lymphadenopathy appreciated on this exam (note: PET-CT did not show cervical nodal involvement at staging).
Skin: Multiple subcutaneous and cutaneous nodules visible and palpable over the trunk (at least 5–6 ranging from 0.5–3.0 cm), bilateral upper extremities (3–4 smaller nodules). Post-biopsy scar on mid-back, well-healing. No ulcerated or bleeding nodules at present. No jaundice, no acanthosis.
Lymph nodes: Right axillary lymph node mass palpable, approximately 4 cm, firm, mildly tender, mobile. Left axillary mild fullness, not frankly nodular to exam. Right inguinal fullness, small palpable node approximately 1.5–2 cm. Left inguinal: borderline fullness.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles or wheeze.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Mild hepatomegaly, liver edge palpable approximately 2–3 cm below the right costal margin, smooth. No splenomegaly. No ascites. No abdominal wall nodules palpable (confirmed subcutaneous on biopsy).
Musculoskeletal: Full range of motion all extremities. No bony tenderness.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact. No focal deficits. Gait normal.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
CBC: WBC 7.8 x10³/µL, Hgb 11.8 g/dL (mild anemia, normocytic), platelets 312 x10³/µL
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 138, Potassium 4.1, Bicarbonate 24, Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL
Liver function: AST 52 U/L (elevated), ALT 47 U/L (elevated), total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 0.3 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 128 U/L (mildly elevated), albumin 4.0 g/dL
LDH: 312 U/L (elevated, approximately 1.5x upper limit of normal — prognostically relevant in metastatic melanoma)
Thyroid function: TSH 2.3 mIU/L (within normal range on current levothyroxine dose)
Coagulation: PT/INR within normal limits
HBsAg: negative; HCV Ab: negative (checked prior to any potential immunotherapy)

Biomarkers (from NGS/pathology, reviewed at this visit):
- BRAF V600E mutation: POSITIVE (allele frequency 48% in tissue; FoundationOne CDx)
- PD-L1 TPS: 15% (companion diagnostic)
- Tumor mutational burden: 28 mutations/Mb (HIGH)
- Microsatellite status: STABLE (MSS)
- Co-occurring molecular alterations: CDKN2A loss-of-function, CDKN2B homozygous deletion, PTEN p.R130G (loss of function), NF1 splice site mutation, TP53 p.R248W (gain of function), TERT promoter c.-124C>T; MCL1 amplification, MYC gain, CDK4 gain; RAC1 P29S
- Germline: BRCA1/2 wild-type; no pathogenic germline variants identified

---

IMAGING RESULTS
PET-CT (whole body with contrast-enhanced CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) — reviewed at this visit:
Primary site: post-biopsy changes at dorsal trunk. Widespread hypermetabolic disease including bilateral axillary lymph nodes (right 4.2 cm, intensely FDG-avid; left 1.8 cm), bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy (right 2.1 cm, left 1.4 cm), and multiple FDG-avid subcutaneous/cutaneous nodules across the trunk and upper extremities (largest 3.1 cm). Hepatic involvement: 6 discrete lesions in both lobes, largest 2.4 cm in segment 6, 5 additional lesions 0.8–1.7 cm. No pulmonary parenchymal disease. No osseous disease.
MRI Brain (with gadolinium): No intracranial metastases identified. Reassuring.

Overall staging impression: Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition) M1c metastatic melanoma with involvement of bilateral nodal basins (axillary, inguinal), diffuse cutaneous/subcutaneous disease, and multiple hepatic metastases. No CNS disease.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

DIAGNOSIS: Stage IV (M1c) metastatic melanoma, BRAF V600E-positive, arising from primary of the mid-back (skin of trunk). High-volume multiorgan disease with bilateral nodal, subcutaneous, and hepatic involvement. No brain metastases at this time. Performance status ECOG 1. Symptomatic from tender right axillary mass, pruritic cutaneous nodules, and moderate fatigue.

MS. OKONKWO presents with newly diagnosed high-burden Stage IV M1c melanoma. Several prognostic factors are worth noting: LDH is elevated at 1.5x ULN (associated with poorer outcomes in metastatic melanoma), she has hepatic involvement (M1c designation), ulceration and high mitotic rate in the primary, brisk TIL infiltrate (potentially favorable immunologically), and a complex molecular profile. The presence of a BRAF V600E hotspot mutation opens the door to oral targeted therapy with BRAF/MEK inhibition, which typically achieves rapid tumor regression and symptom relief, albeit with limited durability. Simultaneously, her high TMB (28 mut/Mb) and moderate PD-L1 expression (15%) make her a reasonable candidate for immunotherapy. The co-mutation pattern on genomic profiling is notable: concurrent PTEN loss and NF1 splice site mutation alongside BRAF V600E is a recognized profile associated with accelerated resistance to BRAF/MEK targeted therapy via PI3K/AKT pathway bypass signaling and RAS-independent MAPK reactivation. This was discussed with the patient.

TREATMENT DECISION:
Given (1) high-volume symptomatic disease with hepatic involvement requiring rapid disease control, (2) BRAF V600E positivity, (3) ECOG 1 with preserved organ function, and (4) patient preference for prompt initiation of treatment rather than enrollment in a clinical trial (clinical trial enrollment was discussed and declined by the patient, who wishes to begin therapy immediately):

RECOMMENDATION: Initiate BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination with dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 150 mg PO BID plus trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg PO once daily. This regimen offers the most rapid disease control option and high objective response rate (~65–70% in BRAF-mutant unresectable/metastatic melanoma), which is appropriate given her symptomatic burden. The plan will be to reassess response at approximately 8 weeks with CT restaging, and to have a detailed discussion regarding the expected duration of response and the strategy for transition to immunotherapy at the time of progression.

IMPORTANT MOLECULAR CONSIDERATIONS: The PTEN loss, NF1 mutation, and TP53 gain-of-function co-mutations on FoundationOne CDx are consistent with a biologically aggressive phenotype. PTEN loss in particular is a recognized mediator of both primary and acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK inhibition. The patient was counseled that median duration of response to targeted therapy in the historical literature is approximately 10–14 months, and that progression is expected; transition to checkpoint immunotherapy (likely combination ipilimumab plus nivolumab given high TMB and moderate PD-L1) is being planned in advance. Clinical trial enrollment will be revisited at time of progression if appropriate options are available.

ADDITIONAL MANAGEMENT:
- Ophthalmology: Baseline ophthalmologic examination scheduled given patient's history of anterior uveitis and the theoretical concern for uveal toxicity with targeted agents; also important as a baseline prior to potential future immunotherapy
- Thyroid: Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH normal at this visit. Will continue to monitor — both BRAF inhibitors and, in the future, PD-1 blockade can affect thyroid function
- Dermatology: Baseline dermatology referral/surveillance established — BRAF inhibitor use is associated with paradoxical MAPK activation in BRAF wild-type keratinocytes, leading to squamoproliferative lesions and cutaneous SCCs; patient counseled and full-body skin surveillance every 8 weeks while on dabrafenib recommended
- Hepatic function monitoring: Baseline liver enzymes mildly elevated (AST 52, ALT 47, ALP 128) in the context of hepatic metastases; will recheck after first 2 weeks on therapy and monitor closely for drug-induced hepatotoxicity vs. disease-related changes
- Supportive care: Anti-emetics and fever prophylaxis counseling provided; patient educated that dabrafenib-associated fever syndrome is common (approximately 25–30% of patients) and instructed to call with temperature >38.5°C
- Social work referral placed for support services and financial counseling regarding oral targeted therapy copay assistance programs
- Patient education: Extensive time spent reviewing diagnosis, prognosis, rationale for treatment sequencing, and immune-related adverse events that may be encountered with future immunotherapy. Patient and partner present; both expressed understanding. Written materials provided.

FOLLOW-UP:
- Laboratory recheck (CBC, CMP, LFTs, LDH, TSH) in 2 weeks after starting dabrafenib/trametinib
- First restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis at approximately 8 weeks
- Return to oncology clinic in 3–4 weeks for toxicity assessment
- MRI brain to be repeated at first restaging given hepatic involvement and M1c stage

Signed: [Attending Medical Oncologist, MD]
Dictated and reviewed by attending
